head.hi: The Director of the Intergovernmental Committee on European Migration (Gibson) to the Secretary of State

div.p: Sir: I have the honour to submit enclosed a memorandum on the U.S. Appropriations rider attached to Public Law 665Mutual Security Act of 1954, enacted Aug. 26, 1954; for text, see 68 Stat. 832. ante, p. 844, the provisions of which make the payments of U.S. contributions to the Migration Committee in 1955 conditional on security clearance of migrants to countries of the Western Hemisphere being based on “reasonable standards”.
In the memorandum I have endeavoured to make some suggestions on general lines for the implementation of the rider which I trust you may consider of some value.

However, after careful study of the subject in the light of the interpretation by the U.S. Congress of the intent and text of the rider, of the tentative plan of its implementation, suggested to me by Mr. George Warren on September 17th, on behalf of the Department of State and of my obligations as Director of the Intergovernmental Committee for European Migration, I find that, before taking further action, I have to ask you for an official interpretation of the rider and for a ruling which would enlighten me on the conditions which the Committee should fulfil in order to qualify for the payments under its provisions.
I have the honour to be, Sir,

head.hi: Memorandum on the U.S. Appropriations Rider Attached to U.S. Public Law No. 665

frus:attachment.p: 1. U.S. Public Law No. 665 appropriates the sum of $10,500,000 under Section 405 (a) as the U.S. Contributions in 1955 to the ICEM (Intergovernmental Committee for European Migration).

A rider attached to the Law makes the payments of these contributions conditional on the security clearance of migrants to countries of the Western Hemisphere being “based on reasonable standards”.The full provision stated: “That no funds appropriated in this Act shall be used to assist directly in the migration to any nation in the Western Hemisphere of any person not having a security clearance based on reasonable standards to insure against communist infiltration in the Western Hemisphere.” (Text of Public Law No. 665, ante, p. 844 attached—Appendix No. 1Six attachments were appended to this memorandum; only Appendix 4 is printed.)

2. Viewed from the angle of the origins, the structure, and the legislation under the terms of which the ICEM has functioned and developed during the first three years of its existence (1952–1954), the provisions of the U.S. Appropriations Rider should be regarded as an additional measure initiated by one of the Member Governments to ensure against further spread and infiltration of communism, rather than as a new departure in the joint anti-communist policy of the 24 Member Governments of the Committee.

3. From the outset the Committee, established under its original name of PICMME (Provisional Intergovernmental Committee for the Movement of Migrants from Europe) at the Conference of 16 Governments held in Brussels in December 1951,See footnote 2, p. 1636. was conceived as an intergovernmental organization entirely free of communist membership and influences. This conception placed the Committee in a category of its own, distinct from all other international organizations. It determined its character and limited its membership to governments of free sovereign nations, directly or indirectly interested in the problems of European international migration and resettlement of refugees and excess populations.

The Brussels ResolutionAdopted at the Conference on Migration, Dec. 5, 1951. defined the kind of migrants to be moved by the Committee in the following terms: “…Ellipsis in the source text. that, consequently,
provisional intergovernmental arrangements between the democratic governments which adopt or may hereafter adopt this resolution are necessary in order to move persons who are attached to the principles to which these governments subscribe and who desire to emigrate to overseas countries where their services can be utilized in conformity with generally accepted international standards of employment and living conditions, with full respect for human rights.”

Legislation passed by the 82nd U.S. Congress in 1952Reference is to the Mutual Security Act of 1952 (Public Law 82–400), enacted June 20, 1952; for text, see 66 Stat. 141. fixed the conditions of U.S. participation in the Committee and of U.S. financial contributions to the Committee’s budgets. (Appendix No. 2)

The provisions of the Brussels Resolution and U.S. legislation reflects the determination of the founders and of the present Member Governments of the Committee not only to safeguard it from ever furthering or assisting the spread of communism but actually show their intention of counter-acting communism by means of assisted, duly controlled international migration.

The specific subject of security clearance of migrants was referred to in the Brussels Resolution of December 5, 1951, which stated that each Member Government was the final judge of measures of clearance of immigrants accepted by its country. Article 7 (a) of the said resolution provides that “each country of reception will retain control of standards of admission and the number of immigrants to be admitted”. This provision is repeated in even more definite terms in the ICEM Constitution (Art. 1, paragraph 2): “The Committee shall recognize the fact that control of standards of admission and the number of immigrants to be admitted are matters within the domestic jurisdiction of States, and, in carrying out its functions, shall conform to the laws, regulations, and policies of the emigration and immigration countries concerned.”

For the ICEM the provision of the rider does not therefore raise a problem of principle but merely of practical application in a way which would enable the Committee to qualify under these provisions for the payment by the U.S. Government of its contribution in 1955.

p.hi: Interpretation of the Provisions of the rider by the U.S. Congress

frus:attachment.p:
4. The necessity for clarification of the intent and text of the rider was raised by Representative Francis E. Walter in the U.S. House of Representatives on August 19th, 1954, in view of ascertaining the position of the Committee under its provisions, particularly those concerning standards of admission of migrants laid
down in the Brussels Resolution and in Article 1, paragraph 2 of the Constitution.

In reply to Mr. Walter’s question and to the doubts expressed by Mr. Celler, Mr. GaryJ. Vaughan Gary (D.). (Representative from Virginia, one of the Conferees responsible for the amended final wording of the rider) and Mr. Taber,John Taber (R.–N.Y.). Chairman of the House Committee on Appropriations and of the Conference, supplied the official interpretation of the rider on behalf of the U.S. Congress and clarified its intent.
[Here follow excerpts from the Congressional Record of August 19, 1954; for the full text of the exchange between Representatives Walter, Gary, Taber, and Celler, see pages 15218–15219.]

This interpretation supplied on behalf of the U.S. Congress by Chairman John Taber and Mr. Gary appears authoritatively to establish that as far as the U.S. Congress is concerned: a)
The rider is not intended to modify or to repeal the Constitution of ICEM. b)
Does not lay down standards of security clearance, but merely asks for “reasonable standards”. c)
Mr. Taber adds “I am satisfied that they (the standards) are going to be provided by any of those countries that have any substantial immigrants.” d)
In conclusion Mr. Gary stated: “that the organisation itself (ICEM) will prescribe the standards and, so long as they are reasonable, they will comply with this provision of the bill.”

p.hi: Plan presented on behalf of the U.S. Department of State to Mr. Gibson, Director of the ICEM by Mr. George Warren, Consultant on Refugees and Migration at the Department of State

frus:attachment.p:
5. Mr. George Warren visited the Director of ICEM in Geneva on September 15th 1954 and, on behalf of the U.S. Department of State, submitted a tentative plan suggesting that Mr. Gibson should seek the authority of the interested Member Governments to approach the Secretary of State and ask him to conclude a contract with the Committee under the terms of which nominal rolls of prospective migrants to the Western Hemisphere would be screened by the security officials of the nearest U.S. Consulates at the cost of the Committee. According to Mr. Warren, the Committee would currently submit to the nearest U.S. Consulates nominal rolls of prospective migrants to Latin American countries. The U.S. Consulates would direct their security officials to make a name check of all such migrants against information available to the U.S. Consulates and, after concluding this procedure, would inform the representatives of the Committee which of the migrants did not qualify. The Migration Committee would move only the migrants
cleared under this procedure. (Mr. Warren’s memorandum of September 17th is attached as Appendix No. 4 and Mr. Gibson’s memorandum of Sept. 16th handed to Mr. Warren is attached as Appendix No. 5.)

At Mr. Warren’s request the Director has given careful consideration to these suggestions in the light of the pertinent provisions of the Brussels Resolution and the ICEM Constitution, of the clarifying Congressional interpretation of the intent and text of the rider reflected in the debate in the U.S. House of Representatives on August 19th and of his obligations, as the elected Director of the Migration Committee, to the Governments of the 24 sovereign nations constituting its membership.

6. On the basis of this study, the Director finds it difficult to reconcile the positions of the Brussels Resolution and the Constitution on security clearance of migrants as well as the interpretation by the U.S. Congress of the intent and text of the U.S. rider, with the procedures suggested in the plan communicated by Mr. George Warren on behalf of the U.S. Department of State.
Under these circumstances, Mr. Gibson considers it necessary and urgent to receive a clear ruling from the U.S. Secretary of State before determining what course to pursue.

7. Analysis of the position of the Committee in regard to the requirements of the rider, as interpreted by the U.S. Congress
a)
Considering that the provisions of the rider do not modify or repeal the Constitution of ICEM, enacted by the U.S. Government in Public Law No. 665, and, particularly Article 1, par. 2 of the Constitution, it is evident that the rider does not impose upon the Administration of the Committee the task of final selection of prospective migrants to countries of the Western Hemisphere. This function continues to be the exclusive prerogative of the receiving countries in accordance with their respective immigration laws and procedures. b)
All Member Governments of the ICEM are equally anxious to prevent the immigration of communists to their respective countries. Their membership in the Committee, based on their acceptance of the terms of the Brussels Resolution and of the Constitution is an effective proof of their unequivocal stand on the prevention of communist infiltration. c)
The immigration laws and regulations of the countries of the Western Hemisphere contain exclusion clauses, some no less specific than those of the United States, on the subject of the admission of subversives. d)
The governments of some of these countries have already taken the necessary measures to implement their laws and regulations by applying reasonable standards of screening of would-be immigrants. e)
Those countries of the Western Hemisphere who have not yet seen their way fully to develop adequate measures of screening of
immigrants for the application of their laws and regulations, would probably welcome helpful advice on practical methods of implementing their own laws and regulations by actually applying “reasonable standards” of security screening of immigrants. (Summary of Immigration Laws and Regulations—Appendix No. 6) f)
On the other hand, the authorities of certain emigration areas, namely Greece and Trieste, are effectively preventing by administrative measures the emigration of persons involved in communist activities. From information available to the Director, it appears that the screening is carried out in these areas under adequate and “reasonable standards”.
In other areas the local authorities, although not in a position to prohibit the departure of subversives, are both competent and willing to furnish all relevant background information at the request of the governments of countries of immigration. g)
It should also be borne in mind that a substantial part of the Committee’s individual migration movements is carried out by major denominational Voluntary Agencies whose determined opposition to communism and communist infiltration is unquestionable.

8. The services of the Migration Committee could usefully be placed at the disposal of Member Governments of countries of emigration and immigration whenever their present practices do not appear fully to implement their respective laws and regulations, to help them set up their own services for security screening of migrants, particularly by facilitating their contact with existing security services, for instance with those of the U.S. or Canada, thereby assisting them to ensure that the standards thus established would meet the requirements of the U.S. rider as interpreted by the U.S. Congress. In the case of countries which for any reason find themselves unable to set up a screening system of their own, the Committee would be ready to offer the services of its competent officials who would communicate to the governments of these countries information on the political reliability of prospective immigrants.

9. After receiving an official ruling from the Secretary of State on the interpretation of the language of the U.S. Appropriations rider, Mr. Gibson would be in a position to explore with the other interested Member Governments the most practical ways and means of implementing its provisions on the lines suggested in the preceding paragraph.
Only then will it be possible for the Director to formulate more concrete and detailed plans.

The Director hopes that a constructive solution, acceptable to all the interested Member Governments, including the U.S. Government, would emerge from his soundings and that full agreement upon the procedures to be adopted will be reached at a private meeting of these governments which could be held during the
Eighth Session of the Intergovernmental Committee at the end of November 1954.

frus:attachment.head: Appendix No. 4This memorandum was enclosed in a letter of the same date from Warren, not printed, which Gibson attached to his letter to the Secretary as Appendix 4.
frus:attachment.p: For: Mr. Hugh Gibson.In a memorandum dated Sept. 16, 1954 (Appendix 5), Gibson asked Warren to summarize in writing the suggestions he had made for dealing with the appropriations rider.

In conversations on September 15th–16th we explored the steps which will need to be taken to enable the Migration Committee to qualify for the full amount of the U.S. contribution for 1955. A limiting condition in the appropriation legislation is to the effect that U.S. funds cannot be used to assist the movement of migrants to the Western Hemisphere who lack a security clearance based on reasonable standards. Security clearance calls for both investigation and evaluation of the information secured.

I stressed the importance of the matter and the urgency of establishing the methods and procedures that will be required. These methods and procedures should be in full operation by January 1, 1955 to avoid any delay in U.S. payments to the Committee or interruption in the movement of the migrants affected. I also suggested that experience under the legislation would be of interest to the Congress in the consideration next spring of the 1956 contribution to the Migration Committee. I offered the cooperation of the Department in assisting the Migration Committee to comply with the intent of the Congress and to accomplish the purpose to be achieved.

In exploring methods, I suggested that the Migration Committee might wish to consider the use of certain U.S. services on a cost-reimbursable basis. This suggestion was put forward not as a formal proposal but as a conceivable arrangement which might be explored further by the Migration Committee and the Department. I observed that the arrangement would have two specific advantages. It would bring into play services already experienced in this field which could be expanded most readily to accomplish the task; costs could be determined rather quickly and kept to a minimum and the operation itself would be expedited from the point of view
of timing. Such an arrangement would also most convincingly establish the fact of compliance with the condition of the legislation.

Without reaching any final conclusion as to the feasibility of this suggestion, we then explored the manner of approach that should be made to the other member governments concerned with the problem. I made the observation that if the condition attached to the U.S. contribution for 1955 were to arise in discussion at the Session of the Migration Committee in November, a prior briefing of the governments as to plans and procedures to be adopted might be useful. We briefly considered the feasibility of notification to the governments through the Chiefs of Mission of the Migration Committee. This procedure presented certain difficulties and we proceeded to explore an alternate possibility of developing the necessary arrangements with governments at the working level in the field. This led us to a discussion of the qualifications of a Security Officer of sufficient experience and capacity to develop satisfactory arrangements. An early appointment of such an officer to proceed on the working level might minimize the danger of misunderstandings at the Session in November [and] the necessity for prolonged conversations on the subject with representatives without prior instructions as to the positions of their respective governments. In concluding the conversations, I again expressed my judgment that the Department would be prepared upon further consultation to support the Migration Committee’s efforts to work out the necessary arrangements with the concerned governments.
